coredata.dc:title: Sensitive detection of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene utilizing fluorescent sensor from carbon dots and reusable magnetic core-shell nanomaterial
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yongli
Zhou, Qingxiang
Wu, Yalin
Li, Shuangying
Sun, Yi
Sheng, Xueying
Zhan, Yali
Zhao, Jingyi
Guo, Jinghan
Zhou, Boyao
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots have been a promising nano-carbon material with many advantages, and attracted many more attentions. This study designed a new chemosensor integrating the strong fluorescent property of carbon dots and the magnetism of amino-functionalized magnetic core-shell nanomaterial, Fe@SiO2–NH2 for determination of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT). In this system, fluorescent carbon dots interacted with amino groups on the surface of amino-functionalized magnetic core-shell nanomaterial leading to fluorescence quenching of carbon dots, appearance of TNT competitively replaced of carbon dots on the surface of the magnetic material through forming a Meisenheimer complex. This sensor exhibits excellent selectivity and sensitivity for TNT, and which provided a good dynamic linear range for TNT from 10 to 2000 ng mL−1. The experiments demonstrate a low detection limit of 2.15 ng mL−1. The intra-day precisions for 25, 100 and 500 ng mL−1 were 4.6, 2.3 and 0.5% (RSD, n = 6), inter-day precisions for 25, 100 and 500 ng mL−1 were 4.2, 2.5 and 0.9% (RSD, n = 6), respectively. The developed sensor was validated with river water, dust, and soil samples, and the achieved spiked recoveries were immensely satisfied from 98.1% to 102.0%. The Fe@SiO2–NH2 possessed excellent reusability. This sensor exhibits that it is simple, sensitive and selective, and will be a vital analytical tool for TNT in many fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemosensor
Fluorescent carbon dots
Amino-functionalized magnetic core-shell nanomaterial
2,4,6-Trinitrotoluene

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The principle and optimization of TNT detection
The precision, stability and the reusability
Practical application of the chemosensor to environmental samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and (B) HRTEM images of CDs (inset: histogram of CDs size distributions). (C) TEM image of Fe@SiO2–NH2. (D) FTIR spectra of CDs. (E) UV–vis absorption (a) and FL (excitation spectrum: b, emission spectrum: c) spectra of the CDs. (F) FL spectra of the CDs obtained at different excitation wavelengths.
Zeta potentials of the CDs solution (1), and CDs + Fe@SiO2–NH2 solution (2), CDs + Fe@SiO2–NH2+TNT solution (3) under the same condition.
FTIR spectra of the CDs + Fe@SiO2–NH2 and CDs + Fe@SiO2–NH2+TNT.
High resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s and O1s of the CDs + Fe@SiO2–NH2 (A) and CDs + Fe@SiO2–NH2+TNT (B).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, CDs + Fe@SiO2–NH2, CDs + Fe@SiO2–NH2+TNT. (B) The optimization of detection system (solvent). (C) The optimization of CDs concentration. (D) The optimization of Fe@SiO2–NH2 dosage. (E) The optimization of incubation time. (F) The optimization of pH (λex/λem = 343/434 nm, CTNT = 1 μg mL−1).

(A) The fluorescence response of the CDs and Fe@SiO2–NH2 system upon the addition of different concentrations of TNT. (B) Linear correlation of ΔF values versus the concentration of TNT over the range 10–2000 ng mL−1. Inset: plot of the fluorescence intensity against TNT concentration within the range of 10–3000 ng mL−1 (λex/λem = 343/434 nm, water and ethanol (1/1, v/v), pH = 7, CCDs = 9 μg mL−1, m Fe@SiO2–NH2 = 10 mg).

Selectivity of the chemosensor for TNT compared to other nitro-aromatic compounds (the blue bars) and the mixture of TNT with nitro-aromatic compounds (the red bars) (λex/λem = 343/434 nm, water and ethanol (1/1, v/v), pH7, CCDs = 9 μg mL−1, mFe@SiO2–NH2 = 10 mg, CTNT = 50 ng mL−1, the concentrations of other nitro-aromatic compounds were all 1 μg mL−1). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Illustration of the structure of the chemosensor and the working principle for the detection of TNT.
Comparison of different chemosensors for the detection of TNT.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: AF MnD ZnS QDs: 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane-functionalized manganese doped ZnS quantum dots creatinine-capped CdSe/ZnS QDs: creatinine-modified CdSe/ZnS quantum dots EuOF and AuNPs: europiumorganic framework (EuOF) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of TNT in real samples.

head.ce:title: Sensitive detection of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene utilizing fluorescent sensor from carbon dots and reusable magnetic core-shell nanomaterial

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots have been a promising nano-carbon material with many advantages, and attracted many more attentions. This study designed a new chemosensor integrating the strong fluorescent property of carbon dots and the magnetism of amino-functionalized magnetic core-shell nanomaterial, Fe@SiO2–NH2 for determination of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT). In this system, fluorescent carbon dots interacted with amino groups on the surface of amino-functionalized magnetic core-shell nanomaterial leading to fluorescence quenching of carbon dots, appearance of TNT competitively replaced of carbon dots on the surface of the magnetic material through forming a Meisenheimer complex. This sensor exhibits excellent selectivity and sensitivity for TNT, and which provided a good dynamic linear range for TNT from 10 to 2000 ng mL−1. The experiments demonstrate a low detection limit of 2.15 ng mL−1. The intra-day precisions for 25, 100 and 500 ng mL−1 were 4.6, 2.3 and 0.5% (RSD, n = 6), inter-day precisions for 25, 100 and 500 ng mL−1 were 4.2, 2.5 and 0.9% (RSD, n = 6), respectively. The developed sensor was validated with river water, dust, and soil samples, and the achieved spiked recoveries were immensely satisfied from 98.1% to 102.0%. The Fe@SiO2–NH2 possessed excellent reusability. This sensor exhibits that it is simple, sensitive and selective, and will be a vital analytical tool for TNT in many fields.

ce:section.ce:para: 2,4,6-Trinitrotoluene (TNT), one of the most well-known and widely used nitroaromatic explosives, has attracted worldwide concern due to the by-products of its degradation that have potential toxicity to all living organisms and pose imminent threat to public security and the environment [1,2]. Numerous methods have been used for the detection of TNT, including chromatography, fluorescence based on water-soluble fluorescent conjugated polymers, photoluminescence based on molybdenum oxide quantum dots electrochemiluminescence aptasensor, photothermal paper sensor, surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), self-assembled molecular gelator, and surface plasmon resonance (SPR), Raman spectroscopy, immunoassay techniques, and electrochemical techniques [3–14]. For example, Wang et al. designed a novel chemosensor for room-temperature phosphorescence sensing TNT based on 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES)-functionalized Mn doped (AF MnD) ZnS QDs [15]. Carrillo-Carrión and coworkers developed a novel method for selectively sensing TNT based on creatinine-modified CdSe/ZnS quantum dots with high sensitivity [16]. Kaur's groups used europium organic framework (EuOF) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) constructing a chemosensor for sensitive detection of TNT [17]. However, most of these detection methods are complicated, time-consuming, and require expensive instrument usage. For many years, significant interest has been focused on the development of easy to operate, cost-effective and sensitive method for determining trace amounts of TNT.

Carbon dots (CDs) have gained enormous attention owing to their unique properties. Compared with other fluorescent sensor based on metal nanoparticles, dyes and quantum dots and on, CDs possess many superior properties, including easy preparation, high solubility in water, highly fluorescent stability, nontoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and environmental-friendliness. Due to such properties, CDs have been effectively utilized in photocatalysis, drug delivery, sensing, and optoelectronic devices [18–24]. The surface of CDs usually has many hydrophilic carboxyl and hydroxyl groups that enable the obtained CDs to exhibit outstanding water-solubility and strong fluorescence and can be also exploited for detecting targets based on changes in the fluorescence intensity [25–28]. Moreover, CDs can be functionalized for selective determination of specific compounds. It is well known that TNT contains three nitro groups which are typical electron-deficient groups, which can selectively form a Meisenheimer complex with primary amines earning electron-rich amino groups via charge transfer during a nucleophilic aromatic substitution reaction, and this selective interaction can be used to establish sensitive determination methods for TNT [29–31]. Zhang and coworkers synthesized amino-functionalized CDs and utilized the strong TNT-amino interaction to measure trace amounts of TNT based on fluorescent quenching from the strong interaction [31]. Meanwhile, such functionalized CDs were used to modify the glassy carbon electrode for developing an electrochemical sensor for TNT, providing sensitivity down to the nanomolar level.

In addition to CDs, magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) have also attracted dramatic interest in recent years due to their extraordinary properties, such as excellent dispersibility, unique magnetic responsivity and facile surface modification [32]. Considering their magnetic properties, MNPs and their modifications with dual-template molecularly polymer, polymethyl methacrylate, and hydrophobic deep eutectic solvent and other materials have been often utilized as good adsorbents for the preconcentration of analytes and elimination of interference from various samples based on magnetic solid phase extraction due to their properties such as ease of separation and high reusability [33–36]. Nanoscale zero-valent iron (NZVI) particles are a new category of magnetic materials that are easily aggregated and oxidized. In order to widen their application scope, functionalized surface modifications such as humic acid, CuO/ZnO and carbon nanotubes, and amino groups have been successfully explored to solve the aggregation and further facilitate the adsorption affinity [37–39]. Based on the properties of CDs and NZVI, present study designed a TNT fluorescent sensor comprised of CDs and amino group functionalized NZVI particles based on “off-on” principle.

Acrylamide, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), (3-Aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES), ortho-nitrophenol (NP), ortho-nitrotoluene (NT) and TNT were purchased from Aladdin, China. Sodium citrate and 2,6-dinitrophenol (DNP) were purchased from J&K Co. Ltd., China. 2,6-dinitrotoluene (DNT) and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, China. FeCl3·6H2O and NaBH4 were supplied by Guangfu Chemical Reagent Company, China. Other reagents used in this experiment were all analytical grade.
The preparation of CDs was similar to the report from our group [28], the parameters in detail and instruments for characterization are seen in supplementary materials.

Fe@SiO2 nanoparticles were prepared as follows: 1.45 g FeCl3·6H2O, 50 mg PVP, 60 mL 70% (v/v) ethanol solution were completely mixed and followed by the addition of 0.2 mL TEOS under ultrasonication with mild nitrogen stream in a three-neck flask. 20 mL NaBH4 (1 M) was titrated into the solution for 4 min at 15 °C, which was kept vigorously stirring for 2 h. The particulate materials were separated and cleaned for several times using ultrapure water and ethanol and then dried under vacuum drying oven. For the synthesis of Fe@SiO2–NH2, 100 mg Fe@SiO2 particles was dispersed into 50 mL isopropyl alcohol containing 200 μL APTES. The mixture was flown with mild N2 stream for 30 min under ultrasonication and followed to heat to 70 °C under mechanically agitating for 6 h. The functionalized particles were magnetically separated, repeatedly washed using ethanol and water by three times, and dried under vacuum drying oven.

UV–vis absorption spectra are measured with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (TU-1810, PGeneral, China). A fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan) is used to measure the fluorescence of the samples. A JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV is utilized to investigate the morphologies of the prepared materials. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis is carried out on a Nicolet Magna-IR 750 spectrometer (Thermo, USA).

10 mg Fe@SiO2–NH2 was weighed and put in a flask (10.0 mL). Then, 30 μL 0.6 mg mL−1 of CDs and 1.97 mL solvent (the mixture of water and ethanol (1/1, v/v)) were added. The final solution was mixed thoroughly at ambient temperature for 1 min. The FL spectra were recorded before and after the addition of Fe@SiO2–NH2. When Fe@SiO2–NH2 was added, after a set interval time, a magnet was put under the bottom of the flask, and the fluorescence intensity of the upper solution was measured. To the above mixture, different volumes of TNT (1, 10, 100 μg mL−1) were added, the solution was diluted to 2.0 mL using the mixture of water and ethanol (1/1, v/v) with the final TNT concentration of 0, 10, 25, 50, 100, 250, 500, 750, 1000, 1250, 1500, 2000, 2500, 3000 ng mL−1, respectively. The change of the fluorescence intensity was monitored. The fluorescence measurements were carried out at room temperature subsequently from 353 nm to 580 nm with excitation wavelength at 343 nm. The excitation and emission slits were both kept at 5 nm and the voltage was 550 V. The selectivity experiments were carried out by adding a certain amount of DNT, NT, TNP, DNP, and NP following the same method described above for TNT.

The pre-treatment of environmental samples was executed according to the literature [40]. As real water samples were concerned, 50.0 mL water was pre-treated with 10.0 mL trichloromethane, and the organic phase was blown to near dryness with nitrogen flow. Then the residue was re-dissolved with 1.0 mL acetonitrile. For soil and dust samples, 1.0 g dry sample was soaked with 10.0 mL acetonitrile by water bath oscillator for 30 min and separated by centrifuging at 3500 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was available for analysis.

Fig. 1 A–D shows the images of transmission electron microscope (TEM), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) of CDs, and TEM image of Fe@SiO2–NH2. Fig. 1B revealed that the CDs are nearly spherical with a small mean size of about 2.4 nm by measuring 120 random nanoparticles. Fig. 1C shows TEM image of Fe@SiO2–NH2, which indicated the size of the formed core-shell structure of about 8–17 nm. The dark core of Fe was clearly observed, and the grey shell was about 2–5 nm in thickness. Fig. 1D exhibited the FTIR spectrum of the CDs (FTIR of Fe@SiO2–NH2, Fig. S1). The strong peaks near 3436 cm−1 and 1410 cm−1 were associated with stretching vibration of N–H/O–H and bending vibration of O–H, respectively. The peak at 1590 cm−1 was assigned to bending vibration of N–H or asymmetric stretching vibration of carboxylate anions. Additionally, the two absorption bands at 1648 cm−1 and 1059 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibration of CO and stretching vibrations of C–N, respectively [28]. Fig. S1 shows the FTIR spectra of Fe@SiO2, and Fe@SiO2–NH2. The bands at 1101, 1089, 798, and 472 cm−1 on Fe@SiO2 and Fe@SiO2–NH2 were attributed to the asymmetric stretching and bending vibrations of SiO2, suggesting successful decoration of silica. Fe@SiO2 was effectively decorated by amino-functionalization proved by the peaks at 3454, 1641, 1461, and 707 cm−1, ascribed to stretching and bending vibrations of amino groups. The appearance of methylene peaks at 2923 and 2854 cm−1 further verified the successful modification by APTES [41]. These results ensured to form a silica shell on the surface of Fe nanoparticles and amino-functionalization. Fig. 1E and F shows that the maximum emission appears at 434 nm when the excitation wavelength is 343 nm. Moreover, the fluorescence emission is independent to the excitation wavelength ranged from 280 to 390 nm. This property which was ascribed to the more uniform size of the CDs, which was in sync with TEM observation as shown in Fig. 1B. Guo's group and Yao's group also reported excitation-independent properties of fluorescence emission of the prepared CDs [42,43]. The quantum yield of CDs was measured to be 18.0% with quinine sulfate (quantum yield 54%) as reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The principle and optimization of TNT detection
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 depicts the architecture of the chemosensor and the detection principle for TNT. In this chemosensor, CDs with high fluorescence were utilized as the fluorophore, and amino groups modified onto NZVI particles were used as the “switch” for the fluorescence quenching of CDs. Upon addition of Fe@SiO2–NH2 nanoparticles into the CD solution, CDs were rapidly adsorbed onto the surface of Fe@SiO2–NH2 nanoparticles due to the electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bonds, which led to the decline of the CDs level, and finally caused the effective decrease of the fluorescence intensity. With the addition of TNT into the CDs and Fe@SiO2–NH2 system, TNT replaced CDs adsorbed on Fe@SiO2–NH2 nanoparticles by forming Meisenheimer complex via selective interaction between methyl groups of TNT molecules and amino groups of Fe@SiO2–NH2. This prompted the adsorbed CDs to release to the solution and restore the fluorescence of CDs. What's more, the fluorescence intensity of CDs kept constant with the addition of TNT without the presence of Fe@SiO2–NH2 nanoparticles. The Zeta potential, FTIR and XPS were further carried out to demonstrate the formation of the Meisenheimer complex. As shown in Fig. 2 , the zeta potential of CDs solution shifted from −9.8 mV to −6.4 mV with the addition of Fe@SiO2–NH2 nanoparticles and then decreased to −8.3 mV in the presence of TNT, which suggested the CDs were firstly adsorbed on the Fe@SiO2–NH2 and then returned to the solution due to the formation the Meisenheimer complex with the addition of TNT. FTIR spectra of the CDs + Fe@SiO2–NH2 and CDs + Fe@SiO2–NH2+TNT were shown in Fig. 3 , the typical characteristic peak was appeared at about 1245 cm−1 and which was associated with the symmetrical stretching vibration of aromatic nitro groups, which further indicated the formation of this complex. As displayed in Fig. 4 , there were some obvious differences between the high resolution XPS spectra of N1s of the CDs + Fe@SiO2–NH2 (A) and CDs + Fe@SiO2–NH2+TNT (B), the peak at 399.2 eV was ascribed to C–N, which also showed the TNT was adsorbed on the Fe@SiO2–NH2 due to the formation of this complex. The above results all demonstrated a Meisenheimer complex was formed via the charge transfer process from an electron-rich amino group in Fe@SiO2–NH2 to an electron-deficient nitro group in TNT [29–31].

Fig. 5 A exhibits that the fluorescence intensity of CDs was observably declined upon the addition of magnetic Fe@SiO2–NH2 nanoparticles into the CDs solution. Moreover, the presence of TNT resulted in the recovery of the fluorescence intensity. For gaining good determination of TNT, some vital parameters, such as the solvent, the initial concentration of CDs, Fe@SiO2–NH2 dosage, the incubation time and the solution pH were examined. In the following experiments for optimization, the spiked concentration of TNT was kept at 1.0 μg mL−1. The ΔF (the difference of F2 and F1) value is utilized for the detection of TNT, in which F1 and F2 are the fluorescence intensities without and with addition of TNT, respectively.

Generally, the solvent played a critical role in the detection process. As exhibited in Fig. 5B, there was a maximum value of ΔF when water and ethanol (1/1, v/v) was used. The value of ΔF reduced with the increase of the water volume. It was possibly because CDs had excellent water solubility and the hydrogen-bond interaction between water and CDs was stronger than that of CDs and Fe@SiO2–NH2 nanoparticles. However, the ΔF also declined with the continue increase of the ethanol volume. So, water and ethanol (1/1, v/v) was employed in the following experiments.

Keeping the concentration of TNT constant, lower level of CDs leads to higher sensitivity, while a high concentration will achieve broader linear range. Fig. 5C indicates that the concentration of CDs put an effective role on determination of TNT. It was observable that the value of ΔF increased when the concentration of CDs increased and then decreased, the value of ΔF achieved a maximal value with the concentration of CDs at 9 μg mL−1. Therefore, 9 μg mL−1 was utilized as the optimal concentration.

Fig. 5D demonstrates the effect of Fe@SiO2–NH2 dosage on the ΔF value. With the increase of Fe@SiO2–NH2 dosage, the adsorbed amount of CDs was also increased, which led to the decrease of fluorescence intensity and enlarged the detection range. However, the larger dosage would offer more interaction points that further lessen the competitive effect between the CDs and TNT and then made the ΔF value reduce. Based on the above results, the Fe@SiO2–NH2 dosage of 10 mg was selected as the optimal dosage.

Fig. 5E depicts the impact of incubation time on the ΔF value. It is obvious that the fluorescence intensity was gradually restored with addition of TNT due to the interaction of TNT and amino groups on the magnetic functionalized nanoparticles. The ΔF value was kept constant over 10 min and then non-observed change was found with prolonging incubation time. In order to ensure the stability of the detection, 12 min was used as the reaction time in the subsequent experiments.

Fig. 5F shows that the ΔF value increased gradually and reached the peak value with sample pH rising up to pH 7.0 and then declined with continuing increase of sample pH. Under strong acidic environment, more hydrogen ions in the solution will interact with the carboxylate groups on the surface of CDs, which enhanced the adsorption effect onto the surface of Fe@SiO2–NH2 and further declined the recovery effect of TNT. Under alkaline environment, the amino groups of Fe@SiO2–NH2 has higher activity which made Fe@SiO2–NH2 own stronger adsorption effect towards CDs and had relatively high recovery effect than that of acidic condition. However, both of the ΔF values were lower than that under the neutral condition. Hence, the solution pH was adjusted to pH 7.0.

When the optimal parameters were determined, the sensing performance of the chemosensor was investigated. Fig. 6 A depicts the change of the fluorescence intensity of the chemosensor with the addition of TNT over a range concentration of 10–3000 ng mL−1 ppb, which clearly indicated that the fluorescence of chemosensor could be effectively resumed by addition of TNT. It also suggested that the interaction between TNT and Fe@SiO2–NH2 was stronger than that between the CDs and Fe@SiO2–NH2 [29–31]. Furthermore, the fluorescence response of the chemosensor had an excellent linear correlation with the concentration of TNT in the range of 10–2000 ng mL−1 (Fig. 6 B). The detection limit for TNT was low to 2.15 ng mL−1 (based on the 3σ/s), which earned similar sensitivity as the previous reports [15–17,42]. Up to now, many researchers have developed many valuable methods for determination of TNT, and they owned their own merits. Wang et al. developed a novel chemosensor for room-temperature phosphorescence sensing TNT based on APTES-functionalized Mn doped (AF MnD) ZnS QDs [15]. The sensor exhibited distinct linearity in TNT concentration range of 11.4–408.6 ng mL−1. Carrillo-Carrión and coworkers developed a novel method for selectively sensing TNT based on creatinine-modified CdSe/ZnS quantum dots [16]. The method had a linear detection range of 10–300 ng mL−1. Kaur's groups used europium organic framework (EuOF) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) constructing a chemosensor for the sensitive detection of TNT [17]. The higher fluorescence quenching was obtained with the increase concentration of TNT and the sensor provided high sensitivity with LOD of 18 ngmL−1. Tian and coworkers prepared paper chemosensor based on CDs@NH2 for TNT and which had been successfully employed to detect TNT in actual ground water [44]. The above-mentioned chemosensors were shown in Table 1 . It was observed that the proposed sensor exhibited many merits including broad linear range and excellent detection sensitivity.

Fig. 7 displays the selectivity of the chemosensor for TNT compared to closely related nitro-aromatic compounds, including DNT, NT, TNP, DNP, and NP. 50 ng mL−1 TNT and 1 μg mL−1 each of the above-mentioned nitro-aromatic compounds were added into CDs and Fe@SiO2–NH2 system, and then the fluorescence intensity (the blue bars) was determined. As exhibited in Fig. 4, no marked changes of ΔF values were found except for TNT. In another series of experiments to analyze fluorescent intensity change, a mixture of 50 ng mL−1 TNT and 1.0 μg mL−1 each of the aforementioned nitro-aromatic compounds was added to the detection system. As shown in Fig. 3 (the red bars), the other coexisting nitro-aromatic compounds exhibited no distinct interference, which powerfully indicated that the proposed chemosensor had a high selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The precision, stability and the reusability
ce:section.ce:para: As a reliable and sensitive determination method was concerned, the precisions and stability of the proposed method were very important. Herein, the parameters, such as the intra-day and inter-day precisions and stability of the method were further performed. In the intra-day and inter-day precision assays, the fluorescence responses of the chemosensor to the concentration of TNT with three levels at 25, 100 and 500 ng mL−1 were recorded. While the fluorescence responses of the chemosensor to the three concentrations of TNT were recorded six times a day for gaining the intra-day precision and continuously measured for six days for obtaining the inter-day precision. The experimental results showed that the intra-day precisions for 25, 100 and 500 ng mL−1 were 4.6, 2.3 and 0.5% (RSD, n = 6), inter-day precisions for 25, 100 and 500 ng mL−1 were 4.2, 2.5 and 0.9% (RSD, n = 6), respectively. What's more, the fluorescence response of the chemosensor to the concentrations of TNT at 100 ng mL−1 was recorded and lasted for 24 h. The results were exhibited in Fig. S2A, and which demonstrated that the ΔF values kept constant within 18 h and a little decline occurred after 24 h.

The reusability of the system was further studied by separating TNT-bound Fe@SiO2–NH2 using an external magnet. In order to investigate the regeneration of Fe@SiO2–NH2 particles, they were washed with 2 mL ethanol five times before reuse in the same detection system. The recovered Fe@SiO2–NH2 was successfully used for ten consecutive cycles, and a very inappreciable loss of sensing capacity was observed in each cycle (Fig. S2B), which suggested that the sensing system had excellent reusability. The experimental results showed that both the precision and stability met with the requirements for TNT analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of the chemosensor to environmental samples
ce:section.ce:para: The high sensitivity and selectivity of the chemosensor towards TNT indicated that it might be directly used for TNT detection in real environmental samples (river water, dust, and soil). The resulted showed that TNT was not detected in blank environmental samples, and the spiked experiments were further carried out. The analytical results of TNT in water samples were seen in Table 2 . It can be seen that the spiked recoveries of TNT were satisfied over the range from 98.1% to 102.0% with RSDs of 0.93–3.5%, which revealed that the chemosensor presented good application potential in environmental monitoring.

In this study, the bule CDs and amino-functionalized magnetic core-shell Fe@SiO2–NH2 nanoparticles have been simultaneously synthesized. Moreover, a novel chemosensor has been successfully developed based on CDs and Fe@SiO2–NH2 nanoparticles for the measurement of TNT by competitive replacement adsorption, which was attributed to the formation of a Meisenheimer complex with interaction between methyl groups of TNT molecules and amino groups on Fe@SiO2–NH2 nanoparticles. The results reveal that this proposed method has remarkable precision, stability, sensitivity, and selectivity, and the sensing system has brilliant reusability. Some vital parameters containing the solvent, the initial concentration of CDs, Fe@SiO2–NH2 dosage, the incubation time and the solution pH were also optimized to gain good detection performance. Under the optimal conditions, the chemosensor had a low detection limit of 2.15 ng mL−1. This chemosensor was evaluated with river water, dust, and soil samples. The spiked recoveries were very satisfied. These results indicated that this chemosensor would be a reliable alternative tool for sensitive detection of TNT in environmental applications with advantages such as simplicity, reliability, low-cost and high selectivity.

Yongli Liu: Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing – original draft preparation. Qingxiang Zhou: Conceptualization, Supervision, Visualization, Investigation. Yalin Wu: Methodology, Software. Shuangying Li: Writing – review & editing. Yi Sun: Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xueying Sheng: Resources, Investigation. Yali Zhan: Writing – review & editing. Jingyi Zhao: Writing – review & editing. Jinghan Guo: Writing – review & editing. Boyao Zhou: Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21976211), Science Foundation of China University of Petroleum-Beijing (No.2462020XKJS04) and Key Scientific Research Project Plan of Henan Province (No. 20A610007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2021.122498.
